mission.title: Install and Configure Envoy on Kubernetes
mission.description: Envoy is a cloud-native high-performance edge, middle, and service proxy that helps manage communication between microservices. Installing Envoy allows you to leverage its advanced features for load balancing, service discovery, and observability in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Envoy Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Envoy Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add envoy https://envoyproxy.github.io/envoy
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Envoy using Helm
steps.description:
Install Envoy in a new namespace called 'envoy-system' with the following command:
```bash
helm install envoy envoy/envoy --namespace envoy-system --create-namespace --version v1.37.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Envoy pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace envoy-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Envoy deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment envoy --namespace envoy-system
```
In the editor, add the following under 'spec.containers.resources':
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Envoy service, set up port forwarding with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/envoy --namespace envoy-system 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Envoy will show the Envoy pods in the 'envoy-system' namespace as running. You can access the Envoy service through port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add envoy https://envoyproxy.github.io/envoy
helm repo update
helm install envoy envoy/envoy --namespace envoy-system --create-namespace --version v1.37.0

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Envoy release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall envoy --namespace envoy-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace envoy-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'envoy-system' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace envoy-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Envoy installation by exporting the configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace envoy-system -o yaml > envoy-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Envoy installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade envoy envoy/envoy --namespace envoy-system --version v1.38.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Envoy pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace envoy-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Envoy pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace envoy-system
``` 
Look for any configuration issues or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Envoy service is not reachable, verify that the port forwarding is set up correctly:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace envoy-system
``` 
Ensure that the correct service name and port are being used for port forwarding.

troubleshooting.title: High resource usage
troubleshooting.description:
If Envoy is consuming too many resources, check the resource limits and requests set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment envoy --namespace envoy-system
``` 
Adjust the resource limits if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Configuration changes not applied
troubleshooting.description:
If changes made to the Envoy configuration are not reflected, ensure that the deployment has been updated:
```bash
kubectl rollout status deployment/envoy --namespace envoy-system
``` 
If necessary, redeploy the configuration.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
